entry.title: Re: Onboarding activation email not getting triggered for...
entry.content:
Check the URL registered on Integration Services Registration Center 

  1. Navigate to the SAP SuccessFactors Admin Center > Integration Services Registration Center screen.

See details at : Manage Real-Time Sync of Users from SAP SuccessFactors to Identity Authentication with Identity Provisioning | SAP Help Portal

The URL format for the SAP Cloud Identity infrastructure: https://<ips-tenant-host>/ipsproxy/service/api/v1/systems/<system-id>

The <system-id> is the ID of the SAP SuccessFactors source system you've added in the Identity Provisioning console and is displayed at the end of the system URL.

entry.title: Re: BAPI_SHIPMENT_CHANGE clean core alternative?
entry.content:
My comment is about BAPI function modules which are released or not.

Many function modules whose name start with BAPI are not available in the BAPI Explorer (transaction BAPI). Only the Business Object Types in transaction SWO1, with at least one released method of type "API" are shown in BAPI.

Also, number of function modules whose name start with BAPI are not released for use by customers.

For instance, 883471 - BAPI_SHIPMENT_CHANGE: Error VTBAPI 227 - SAP for Me: "Caution
The BAPI BAPI_SHIPMENT_CHANGE is not released officially. So the usage of this BAPI is on your own risk.".

Originally, the "release" attribute is available in the attributes of the function modules:

Now, this attribute is in the API state via Eclipse ADT:

Example of released BAPI (BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2):

For information, the old release information is stored in the tables RODIR and in TFDIR-FREEDATE (for function modules), the new release information is stored in the table ARS_W_API_STATE. It can be fun to do a query and see how many BAPI are released or not, those which were released originally and the ones now released, etc.

entry.title: Re: MvT 561 Reversal with Partial Consumption and Closed...
entry.content:
This is a tricky situation because you have three constraints at once:

  • The wrong movement type (561 instead of 712) was posted.
  • The posting was duplicated.
  • Part of the stock has already been consumed, and the FI period is closed.

Standard SAP Considerations

Direct reversal of 561 (MvT 562)
Normally, you would reverse a 561 posting with 562. But since part of the stock is already consumed, you cannot fully reverse the quantity. Only the remaining stock can be reversed.

Closed FI period
Any reversal posting will hit accounting. Since the period is closed, you cannot post back into January 2026. You must post in the current open period (February 2026). This will create a timing difference in stock valuation.

Partial consumption
For the consumed portion, you cannot reverse directly. Instead, you need to adjust stock via an inventory adjustment (MvT 701/702) or a physical inventory process to correct the quantities. This ensures ERP stock matches the actual warehouse situation.

Best Practice Approach

  • Step 1: Reverse the remaining stock with MvT 562 in the current open period.
  • Step 2: For the consumed portion, perform a physical inventory adjustment (701/702) to correct the stock.
  • Step 3: Document the correction clearly for audit purposes, since the FI period mismatch will show up in reports.
  • Step 4: If required, use note postings or manual FI adjustments to align financials, depending on your company’s accounting policy.

entry.title: We Want to Hear from You: Comment on Finance Documentation
entry.content:
We've made it easier for you to share feedback on our product assistance documentation. Starting with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, you can now comment directly on documentation for several Finance subareas, such as:

  • General Ledger Accounting  
  • Cash and Liquidity Management  
  • Receivables Management  
  • Invoice Management 
  • Divisional Accounting 
  • Overhead Accounting 
  • Inventory Accounting 
  • Production Accounting 
  • Sales Accounting 
  • Predictive Accounting 
  • Financial Planning 
  • APIs for General Ledger Accounting 
  • APIs for Cash and Liquidity Management 
  • APIs for Receivables Management 
  • APIs for Invoice Management 
  • APIs for Management Accounting and Margin Analysis 

Why This Matters

Good documentation makes all the difference in your daily work. Whether you've spotted something unclear, found a gap, or simply want to suggest improvements, we want to know. Your input doesn't just improve a page — it helps everyone who comes after you.

How It Works

Head to the relevant documentation page, and you'll find the commenting option ready to use. For detailed instructions on how to provide documentation feedback, see Giving Documentation Feedback on SAP Help Portal | SAP Help Portal.

What Happens Next

Your comments go directly to our documentation team. We review every piece of feedback and use it to make real improvements.

Your Voice Shapes Better Documentation

Ready to make an impact? Visit the documentation pages and share your first comment. We're listening and we truly appreciate hearing your insights!

Related Links

Comment Directly on User Guide for SAP Green Ledge... - SAP Community

entry.title: Start Implementing SAP EWM with this free course from SAP
entry.content:
What You’ll Learn in “Basic Customizing in SAP S/4HANA EWM”

If you’re planning to implement SAP EWM, this course is designed for practical application and  understanding the system environment to implementing key warehouse processes and basic integration with SAP ERP. In this blog I’ll take you through the structure of the course and how to expand on these skills.

 In the “Applying Basic Customizing in SAP S/4HANA EWM” course, you’ll learn to configure and apply essential implementation steps, including integrating SAP EWM with SAP ERP, creating product and packaging master data, defining warehouse structures and process types, implementing putaway and pick strategies, handling stock movements (postings, transfers, replenishment), and running basic physical inventory and post-processing tasks. The course does not cover advanced processes such as wave management.

Course Structure

The course contains 11 units and should take roughly six hours of focused lessons. Below is a breakdown:

  • Unit 1 — System environment
    Understand EWM’s place in the S/4HANA landscape: deployment options, architecture, and how embedded EWM differs from decentralized setups.
  • Unit 2 — Setting up integration
    Learn how to connect SAP ERP and EWM so that documents (deliveries, purchase orders) and master data flow correctly. Integration is foundational—without it, core inbound and outbound processes won’t function.

 

  • Unit 3 — Structural elements and master data
    Configure warehouses, storage types, sections, bins, product master data, and packaging specifications. Proper master data and structure ensure accurate stock visibility and predictable warehouse behavior.
  • Unit 4 — Warehouse process types
    Define standard process types used to trigger warehouse tasks and document flows. These are the building blocks for inbound and outbound logic.
  • Unit 5 — Receiving goods
    Covers goods receipt processes: direct and rule-based putaway, capacity checks, and posting goods receipts (GR) in embedded EWM. This unit shows how to get products into the right places efficiently.
  • Unit 6 — Shipping goods
    Focuses on outbound flows and picking strategies—critical for on-time delivery and accurate shipments.
  • Unit 7 — Storage control
    Introduces storage control configuration to map ERP warehouse tasks to physical activities and to control bin-level movements.
  • Unit 8 — Creating warehouse orders
    Learn how warehouse orders are created and managed—central for coordinating work across resources and tasks.
  • Unit 9 — Posting changes, stock transfers, replenishment
    Covers the mechanics of moving stock between types and statuses, posting corrections, and setting replenishment triggers—day-to-day operations you’ll configure and support.
  • Unit 10 — Physical inventory
    Covers configuration and execution of inventory counts and adjustments—essential for auditability and accurate stock.
  • Unit 11 — Post Processing Framework
    Provides an overview of PPF to automate follow-up actions (notifications, status changes) after key events.

Why these skills matter in practice

1. Faster, smoother go-lives: solid basics reduce rework during detailed configuration and testing.
2. Fewer operational exceptions: well-defined putaway/pick rules and capacity checks minimize manual interventions.
3. Better integration: a correct ERP–EWM setup reduces document mismatches and stock inconsistencies.

 

Tips to get the most from the course
  • Practice in a sandbox system: configuration concepts become clear when you try them.
  • Map course concepts to your warehouse processes: draw current vs. target flows to see where settings apply

After completing the basic course, you can advance to topics such as labor and resource management, wave and yard management, automation, and more extensive integration within the Business Suite. Links to these courses can be found here.

Wrapping Up

Completing the SAP EWM basic course gives you a solid foundation in warehouse management fundamentals, practical configuration skills, and hands-on processes you can apply on a project. With this base, you’ll be better equipped to set up SAP EWM basics. Whether you’re upskilling for implementation skills or preparing your team for digital transformation, this course is a practical first step toward greater efficiency and control in your warehouse operations. In my next blog, I’ll cover advanced topics and real-world implementation tips.

For more information on SAP S/4HANA EWM, see the community page set up by the product management team at SAP: https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/extended-warehouse-management

Here is a list of informative implementation guides for SAP S/4HANA EWM: https://dam.sap.com/mac/app/p/pdf/asset/preview/hv2DfQT?ltr=a&rc=10&doi=SAP894096

entry.title: Re: VSCode - Unable to deploy or preview to ABAP on-prem...
entry.content:
Try this link https://github.com/SAP-samples/fiori-tools-samples/tree/main/misc/proxy
If you are getting HTTP 407 that is being returned from your local proxy and is requiring credentials, these credentials will be different or at least should be different to your ABAP backend.

Setting npm proxy will not resolve your issue with HTTP requests, this requires your OS env variables to be configured with the proxy URL and proxy credentials.

John

entry.title: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA Cloud – New User Experience Switch Enhanced Explained
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the New User Experience Switch has been enhanced to make change even easier for you and your business users.

Examples of New Version Switch messages

Find out how:

  • The New User Experience Switch makes change easier
  • What’s New in the New User Experience Switch for 2602
  • What you need to do as a business user
  • What you need to do as an administrator
  • FAQ

 

Making change easier with New User Experience Switch

“Progress is impossible without change.” George Bernard Shaw

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition, you receive the latest innovations every 6 months. 

With each new release, new apps are phased-in and sometimes old apps may need to phased-out.

For business users that means moving from an old app to the new app that replaces it.

You will have at least 12 months (2 x releases) to make the shift.   Sometimes that 12 months can pass very quickly.  It can help to have a little push in the right direction to get you started.

The New User Experience Switch makes change easier by:

  1. Letting you know a new app is being phased-in
  2. Encouraging you to try out the new app
  3. Warning you when the old app is being phased-out
  4. Giving you a gentle push to move to the new app before time is up!

You only see the New User Experience Switch if you are using an app that is planned to be phased-out.

How does the New User Experience Switch work in 2602

The New User Experience switch was first introduced with release 2502 and has been enhanced in releases 2508 and 2602.

The New Experience Switch consists of a popover displayed when launching the old app and a toggle button in the shell bar that allows users to switch between the old and new app. The switch changes appearance depending on the stage of the app replacement to an Information, Warning, or Alert message.

Information 

In this stage the New Experience Switch lets users know that a new app version is being phased-in, explains its benefits in comparison to the old one and encourages users to try it. 

New User Experience Switch - Information message example

If you like the new app, you can opt-in to start using it immediately.  During phase-in, you can switch back to the old app at any time.

Warning 

As soon as the old app is to be phased out (also known as deprecated), the New Experience Switch changes to the Warning mode to let users know that the app will be removed in an upcoming release. 

Example of a New Version Warning message

Alert

In the last release before the old app is finally removed, the New User Experience switch indicates the urgency for users and shows when the app will be removed.

Example of a New Version Alert message

IMPORTANT: Once the app has been removed, you must move to the new app.

With release 2602, if an old app is being split across multiple new apps, during phase-in and phase-out the New User Experience helps you choose by explaining the choices.

Example of New Version available, warning, and alert messages where there are 2 new apps replacing an old app

What you need to do as a business user

Make sure you use the option to give feedback on the new app especially if you notice something is missing!  SAP product managers are making decisions based on your feedback.  For example, they may delay the phase-out to the next release, while the missing feature is resolved, or new extension options are added.

Do NOT leave giving your feedback until the Alert stage!  By then it may be too late. Silence is consent!

There’s two ways to provide feedback on the new app:

1. Toggle back from the new app to the old app
If you are in the new app and toggle back to the old one, a push survey comes up asking you for your feedback. Especially interesting for SAP product teams are the comments regarding missing features or suggestions for improvements.

Example of the Feedback Survey

2. Use the Provide Feedback button in the Welcome popover in the new app
You can directly access the push survey from the “Welcome” popover available in the new app. After launching the new app, click the icon next to the toggle to display the “WeIcome” popover and use the button “Provide Feedback”.

Provide Feedback button in the Welcome popover for the new app

 

Make sure you use the option to give feedback if you have any concerns and especially notice if something is missing! 

Product managers are making decisions based on your feedback.  For example, they may delay the phase-out to the next release, while the missing feature is resolved, or new extension options are added.

IMPORTANT: Do NOT leave giving your feedback until the Alert stage!  By then it may be too late. Silence is consent!

What you need to do as an Administrator

During phase-In, make the new app available to business users so they can try ir out early, and opt-in to the new app if they wish.

  • HEADS-UP: Starting with release 2602, new apps (delivered for the first time in 2602) are delivered as inactive (due to the underlying IAM apps delivered as inactive). This means, that the new experience switch for these app replacement cases is initially not displayed (even if the new app is delivered in the same business catalog). You need to activate the new successor apps first, before the New Experience Switch is displayed to your business users.
  • You can find more information in the 2602 - What’s New in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • You can find a list of the impacted apps in 2602 in SAP Note 3545277 - New Experience Switch in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Overview of Apps  in the Attachments section. 

During phase-out, you need to plan for replacing old apps with the new apps in your next release upgrade, and remind your users of which apps are due to be replaced.

For more advice on what to do during release upgrades, refer to:

  • SAP Help – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Identity and Access Management – App Replacement and Deprecation 
  • SAP Help – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Upgrade phase -  Manage App Replacements and Deprecations

You should also know that once the old app reaches the warning (deprecated) phase:  

  • If you are a new customer joining SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you will be moved to the new app immediately. The old app will not be available to you. This means the New Experience Switch will not be displayed to your users.
  • If you are an existing customer, move to the new app as soon as possible.  If you have decided to switch to the new app for your entire company, you can stop the appearance of the switch by deactivating the IAM apps of the old app. With this you stop the availability of the old app for your users, and this also turns off the New Experience Switch for that app.

For more on what’s changing in the latest release, view the SAP Community topic page for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

FAQ

How do I avoid or minimize getting the New User Experience message

No-one likes too many reminders. 

For business users, the popover is only automatically expanded if you launch the old app. Launching the new app displays the toggle, but the popover is not automatically expanded.

The frequency of the automatically expanded popover differs according to the stage.

  • During the phase-in of the new app, you can use the option Don’t show this again to stop the expansion for an entire release.
  • During the phase-out the urgency to switch to the new app increases to keep you reminded of the need for change. You can use the option Remind me Later to postpone the popover appearance for one month (Warning) or one week (Alert).

 

When is the best time to give feedback on the new app

You can send SAP feedback at any time by using the feedback icon (see below) in the shell bar at the top of the launchpad. Select the icon to give feedback on the current app.     

Good to know: The Switch and its latest features were based on User Research feedback with some of our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customers – just like you! If you would like to get involved in future changes you can reach out through our SAP Customer Engagement Initiatives on the SAP Community topic Customer Influence and Adoption.

entry.title: Re: BAPI_SHIPMENT_CHANGE clean core alternative?
entry.content:
@Dominik_Tylczynski I don't have an opinion if @sh4il3sh should replace the unreleased function.

I know a lot of companies have a policies that does not allow usage of unreleased functions and the question was if there is an alternative. There is an alternativ - IDOC.

I guess it would be possible to call the function IDOC_INPUT_SHPMNT directly as the me.sap.com AI suggested and thus make the process synchronous, but that would be stupid because IDOC_INPUT_SHPMNT is also not released.

In that way the AI suggest to avoid calling one unreleased function by calling another also unreleased function. 🤣

entry.title: Re: not able to create tax code
entry.content:
1. Align with SAP’s delivered tax codes and use SSCUI to configure rates.
  2. Avoid trying to replicate on‑premise numbering logic; in Public Cloud, numbering is controlled and standardized.
  3. Communicate to your client that in Public Cloud, tax code numbers are system‑driven identifiers, not business‑driven labels. The business meaning should be carried in the description, not the number.

entry.title: Managing Joule on SAP BTP: Roles, Analytics, Administration and Troubleshooting
entry.content:
Introduction

As SAP continues to roll out Joule integrations across multiple LoBs, with stronger and wider SAP and non-SAP ecosystem bringing the powerful AI-driven assistance, Agents, Agents to Agents, and many more functionalise to our business users. To ensure these functionalities run smoothly, every organization requires the right administrative setup and awareness of key configurations; at the same time it becomes increasingly important to understand how to manage, troubleshoot, understand the consumption patterns in your organisation with most frequently used questions driving your organizational process are some of the key features, and enhance the digital assistant’s functionality efficiently.

This blog aims to help administrators and support colleagues navigate the essential aspects of managing Joule service within an SAP Business Technology Platform environment. It walks through the available roles and role collections, their purposes, and how to configure them effectively.

The blog also focuses on troubleshooting best practices — highlighting the common checks, logs, and details that SAP Support typically requests during issue resolution. By following these guidelines, you can resolve common problems faster, share the right information upfront, and reduce back-and-forth communication with SAP.

Ultimately, this guide aims to make your Joule operations more efficient, your issue-handling more structured, and your collaboration with SAP smoother and quicker.

Before we get started, it’s important to understand the overall Joule integration for your organization. Please look at the blogs below:

This blog post is a series for Joule Activation:

  • SAP Business AI – Overview for all !!! (Includes information on Joule Supported Data Centers, Different scenarios during Joule setup, etc..)
  • Joule - Unified Setup: Bridging Simplicity and Performance (Demo Video to understand the Unified Setup, Joule basics & architecture, how to set up Joule, etc...) 
  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide
  • Joule Setup – End-to-End Setup Guide (for all Line of Business) Unified Approach 
  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Setup Guide
  • SAP Digital Supply Chain
    • Joule for SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) – Setup Guide
    • Joule Setup for SAP Integrated Product Development
  • Joule – Getting Started with Document Grounding - setup guide
  • Activate Joule with SAP Build Work Zone and SAP Mobile Start
  • Analytical Insights
    • Analytical Insights in Joule – Setup Guide
  • Joule Integration with SAP Ariba - Setup Guide
  • Joule Integration with SAP Concur - Setup Guide
  • Joule integration with SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite – Setup Guide

 

👉Managing Joule on SAP BTP: Roles, Analytics, Administration and Troubleshooting - you are here

Blogs - Work in Progress (WIP):

  • SAP Digital Supply Chain (WIP)
    • SAP Asset Performance Management
    • SAP Product Lifecycle Management
    • SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • and more to be added towards General Availability

****************************************************************************

This blog explains the key features and administration aspects of Joule on SAP BTP.

  • Joule Features
    • Downloading traces for debugging and SAP support
    • Conversation search across chat history
    • Version details, support components, and personalization settings
  • Roles and Access Management
    • Available Joule roles in SAP BTP
    • Creating and managing role collections
    • Assigning roles to users and user groups
    • End User access
    • Tenant Admin and Joule Admin Center
    • Extensibility Developer capabilities
  • Admin and Analytics Interfaces
    • Joule Analytics Center for usage statistics and trends
  • Conversation History FAQs
    • Active and expired conversations
    • Retention limits and lifecycle behaviour

Disclaimer:
This blog is meant to provide a general understanding and practical guidance for working with SAP Joule based on common scenarios. For detailed and up-to-date information, please always refer to the Joule | SAP Help Portal.

1. General Features – Download Traces, Conversation Search, etc.

 1. 1 Download Traces

One of the important things to make note of is that one can download traces which can be attached in case of any errors/support required from the team. This option enables the user to be quick in providing the information for debugging which would be required by the SAP Support teams. To download traces, just click on Settings, in the pop-up, go to About and click Download Traces.

Image 1

Another pop-up will come, where you can choose whether to include message history in the trace files. Once done, click on Download and you’ll have a .zip file downloaded. This file can be attached in the support ticket created to SAP in case of any errors faced while using Joule.

Image 2

The file downloaded can contain the following details

  • Joule version and environment details.
  • UI Logs and the most recent 5-10 HTTP requests.
  • Correlation IDs for the last 5-10 messages that can be used by SAP to fetch additional details logged by SAP.
  • Message history as a JSON file (included only based on user consent).

Find the support Components to which the ticket has to be created for specific joule issues in the below table.

CA-JOULE

Joule

CA-JOULE-CI

Joule Conversation Logging and Insights

CA-JOULE-CLI

Command Line Interface tool

CA-JOULE-CLT

Joule Messaging Client-related topics

CA-JOULE-CLT-COP

Specific for Joule x Copilot Integration

CA-JOULE-COMP

Joule Compiler

CA-JOULE-CONSULT

Joule Consulting

CA-JOULE-CR

Joule Conversational Runtime

CA-JOULE-PRV || CA-JOULE-PRV-INT

Provisioning & Integrations with applications

CA-JOULE-TA

Tenant Administration - Joule Content Deployment related issues

CA-JOULE-TR || CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

Tenant Runtime (Bot-runtime), Joule Subscription, SPR, TO and Approuter issues

BC-CP-MP-FOR

Unified Customer Landscape (UCL)

BC-IAM-IDS

IAS

For further information, you can refer to the help portal - Monitoring and Troubleshooting | SAP Help Portal.

1.2 Conversation Search

Conversation search allows users to quickly find past conversations (including expired conversations) from their history.

From the side navigation panel, click on the search icon to access conversation search.

Image 3

Enter one or more keywords to search across your conversations.

Image 4

Search results are dynamically updated as you type. Matching strings are highlighted in the results obtained from the search. The results returned include both the conversations where the title may contain the search term as well as those where the search term is found in any of the messages in the conversation. Selecting a search result navigates you directly to the matched conversation and message.

 1.3 Other features

User can also check the version of Joule and the Support Component to raise tickets for Joule in Setting > About.

Image 5

In Settings > Personalization, you can choose to disable streaming and whether to turn off animations.

Image 6

2. Joule Roles

Prerequisite: You should be a subaccount admin.

As an administrator, you’ll be able to view the available roles and role collections in your subaccount. Open the SAP BTP Cockpit and go to your global account and subaccount.

  • Choose Security > Roles
  • Search for “das” in the search bar.
  • You’ll see the list of the available roles for Joule under the Application Name das-application

Image 7

2.1 Create Role Collections, Assign Roles and Users

In order to assign the roles to users or user groups, we need to first add them into role collections as these roles are not part of any collection and cannot be assigned to users unless we create the collection. To create a role collection (screenshot attached),

  • Go to Security > Role Collections
  • Click on Create, enter a name for the role collection and click Create.

Tip: You can decide to create one common role collection for all Joule roles and assign them together or create separate role collection in case you want have better controls and maintain it individually.

Image 8

The new role collection will get listed in the page, now select the created role collection, and click on Edit.

Image 9

 

 Now, under Roles, click on the textbox, a pop-up will appear.

Image 10

 

Search for “das-application” in Application Identifier. Select the appropriate option. You’ll see the Joule roles listed. Select the ones which must be added and click on Add.

Image 11

 

This helps us to create the Role collections which now can be assigned to the users.

To assign users, go to the users section under the role collection, enter the email-id and select appropriate identity provider. Now you can Save the role collection.

Image 12

Now let’s go to our joule and see what options are available as a user with the role “End User”.

2.1.1 End User (end_user)

This is the role which gives the user access to the standalone URL of your Joule tenant. For more on using the joule web client, refer Using the Joule Web Client | SAP Help Portal.

2.1.2 Tenant Admin (tenant_admin)

As a tenant admin, you can access the Joule Admin Center - a tenant specific user interface, offered for customizing respective Joule configurations. You can find more information here – Joule Admin Center | SAP Help Portal.

To access the Joule Admin Center:

Go to the BTP Cockpit and open your subaccount having Joule. Go to Subaccount Overview > Instances and Subscriptions.
Expand the Joule application subscription (das-application). Click Go to Application to fetch the Joule application URL.

Image 13

Get the Joule tenant URL and append "/admin" to form the admin center URL for the respective Joule tenant - <Joule tenant URL>/admin.

Image 14

You’ll get the below page for Joule Admin Center, given you have the role tenant_admin assigned to your user. Here you can find the list of assistants available in your Joule, and you can select each of them to see their system alias and destinations setup for each.

Image 15

 

Note: SAP team is actively working to add more details and features to this page, stay tuned for more updates. In your case you may only see the “sap_digital_assistant” with standard Joule integration.

2.1.3 Extensibility Developer (extensibility_developer)

This one is applicable to the customers/partners who would like to use Joule extensibility in your landscape. This role is required to build and deploy capabilities for Joule. For more information, refer Joule Development | SAP Help Portal.

In addition, this role provides Debug option which provides visibility into runtime execution through the Logs View, which helps administrators and developers monitor and troubleshoot custom capabilities effectively.

With this role, users can:

  • View end-to-end execution logs for Joule conversations.
  • Easily identify execution outcomes using visual status tags (Blue for success, Red for errors).
  • View Correlation IDs to trace requests across the complete conversation and use these as supporting information for issue analysis and support ticket creation.

Use log details as To use the Debug features, open the Joule conversation window, click on the Debug icon on the top right corner. A side window named “Request Logs” will appear, Here you can find the logs and trace the conversation.

Image 16

A side window named “Request Logs” will appear, Here you can find the logs and trace the workflow of the conversation in case you face any error or run into an issue. You can expand the log messages by clicking on the arrow beside the message to find further details.

Image 17

Now, let us see how to fetch the Correlation ID for a conversation in Joule. This ID must be shared with the support team for effective troubleshooting and error tracing.

Open the conversation for which you want to retrieve the Correlation ID. For messages generated by Joule, a debug icon is displayed in the top-right corner of the message. This icon will be blue if the response was generated successfully. In case the request ran into an error, the icon will be red.

Image 18

Click this icon to open a split view titled “Response Payload.” Scroll to the bottom of the payload, where you will find the correlationID field.

Image 19

Share this Correlation ID with the Joule support team to assist with issue investigation and analysis.

Note: For every conversation where Joule is not working as expected or run into any errors, the Joule Support team will be requesting the correlation ID or the conversation trace. Please ensure to have these information attached to the support ticket which you’ll be creating for the issues/troubleshooting.

2.1.4 Analytics Admin (analytics_admin)

Joule offers an analytical insight on the Joule usage with the analytics_admin role. You can find the official help page here – Joule Analytics Center | SAP Help Portal.

Go to the BTP Cockpit and open your subaccount having Joule. Go to Subaccount Overview > Instances and Subscriptions.

Expand the Joule application subscription (das-application). Click Go to Application to fetch the Joule application URL.

Image 20

Get the Joule tenant URL and append “/analytics” to form the Joule Analytics Center URL for the respective Joule tenant (your BTP subaccount) - <Joule tenant URL>/analytics.Image 21

You’ll get the below page of Joule Analytics Center. Click Acknowledge and continue to explore the analytical insights provided.

Image 22

You can view analytics across interaction types, products, scenarios, and client types for a selected time range, providing insights into overall Joule usage, adoption, and engagement.

The Summary section highlights key metrics, including total messages, total conversations, and the average messages per conversation, giving a quick snapshot of overall interaction volume and conversation depth.

Image 23

We have 2 sections, Messages and Conversation - each representing a different level of interaction. A message is an individual request or response generated within Joule and is counted across all conversations. A conversation represents a single, distinct interaction thread initiated by a user, identified by its conversation topic name. Each conversation can contain one or more individual messages exchanged within that thread.

The Messages section breaks down message activity by product, scenario, and interaction type, helping you identify which products and scenarios are most frequently used and how users interact with Joule across different interaction types.

Image 24

The Product Usage graph clearly shows which products are using Joule most actively. From the sample data in the screenshot below, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud has the highest usage, followed by SuccessFactors and SAP Analytics Cloud / Just Ask, while products such as IBP and IPD show comparatively lower message volumes. This view helps identify where Joule adoption is strongest and enables easy comparison of usage across different products.

Image 25

The Scenario Usage graph shows how frequently different scenarios are used in Joule. From the data, scenarios such as “ESH_U_SALESORDER” and “purchase order–related scenarios” have higher usage. Other scenarios, including employee views, feedback, and scheduling workflows, show comparatively lower usage. This view helps identify the most actively used scenarios and supports prioritization of monitoring and optimization efforts for high-impact workflows.

Image 26

The Interaction Type Usage graph illustrates how users engage with Joule across different interaction types. The data shows that scenario-based interactions account for most of the usage, followed by direct responses and slot filling, while information retrieval and file uploads contribute only a small portion. This view helps understand preferred interaction patterns when interacting with Joule.

Image 27

You can also find weekly statistics of messages and conversations.

The Message Count Over Time graph shows how the total number of messages sent to Joule varies over the selected time range. From the sample view, you can see clear peaks and drops, indicating periods of higher and lower activity. This helps administrators and the organization to identify usage trends, understand peak activity periods, and correlate spikes with specific events, releases, or user activity.

Image 28

The Conversation Count Over Time graph displays how the number of unique conversations changes over time. In the sample screenshot, you can observe days with higher conversation counts, indicating increased user engagement, followed by periods of lower activity. Comparing this graph with the message count helps distinguish between longer conversations and an increase in the number of users or sessions, providing better insight into overall usage behavior.

Image 29

You can also find Product Usage and Scenario usages under the Conversations showing which product/scenario generate the most conversations.

Image 30

Additionally, there is a Client Type Usage graph which shows the number of conversations across different client types, such as Desktop, Mobile, and Copilot, helping you understand how conversations are distributed by access channel.

Image 31

 Overall, these graphs make it easy to visually identify where usage is highest, compare adoption across products, scenarios, and interaction types, and gain actionable insights for monitoring and operational analysis.

 3. FAQs on Joule Conversation History

  • Adding, deleting, and renaming conversations
    • Create a new conversation by selecting the "New Conversation" option from the side panel menu.

Image 32

  • When a new conversation is created, a conversation title is automatically generated based on the first user request–response in the conversation thread.
  • You can rename a conversation at any time by hovering over the conversation in the side panel and selecting "Rename" from the conversation menu, or by selecting Rename from the conversation header.

Image 33

  • To delete a conversation, hover over the conversation in the side panel or open the action menu from the conversation header, then select Delete. A pop-up will appear, confirm by clicking “Delete”

Image 34

Image 35

  • Active conversations
    • A maximum of 10 active conversations can be maintained at any time. This helps users keep conversations segregated and organized, for example by using separate conversations for different applications such as SAP S/4HANA and SuccessFactors, or by specific business scenarios like leave requests, travel requests, and other workflows.
    • Conversations are sorted with the most recently active on top.
    • Switch between conversations to continue engaging in a different conversation.

Image 36

 

Image 37

  • Expired conversations
    • A conversation expires after 8 hours of inactivity. Upon expiration you'll be notified and provided with an option to trigger a new conversation.
    • Conversations are sorted by the creation date of the thread with the newest at the top.
    • Conversations are deleted automatically 7 days after the date of creation.
    • Expired conversations cannot be continued. They can only be referenced for past interaction history.

Image 38

 

As Joule adoption grows across products and scenarios, effective administration becomes the backbone of a reliable user experience. With a clear understanding of Joule roles, analytics, and troubleshooting tools, administrators can proactively monitor usage, resolve issues faster, and support business users more efficiently.

Credits & Special thanks to RIG Team (Nagesh Caparthy & Naga Swathi T J) for supporting this blog post. 

Until the next update — stay tuned and happy learning!!!

Best Regards,
Amrita Varshini E R
SAP Business AI RIG Team

entry.title: Configure Web GUI for HTML in Build Work Zone, standard edition
entry.content:
Hello Everybody,

 

Writing this blog to explain how we can access the SAP Web GUI for HTML (Home Page of GUI) of S/4HANA On Premise (SAP Rise) from SAP BTP Build Work Zone. SAP S/4HANA Rise system is a private cloud example, we can use public cloud also.

Here we are using SAP BTP Build Work Zone, Standard Edition. You can also use SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition also.

Prerequisites: Below are the prerequisites before we start:

-> Within the global account, you should subscribed to SAP Build Work Zone, standard edtion Service and Configured the Cloud Identity Service. The Work Zone will use Identity Authentication Service (IAS) to authenticate the Business Users.

-> In the Cloud Connector, configure the backend s/4hana system with https port for secure tunneled access and exchange the certifiate for Principal Propagation (The user of PP should be same in IAS and S4)

-> Destination should be there in the Sub account and choose the Principal Propagation as authentication.

 

Configuration:  There are 2 ways to configure the application, Page, spaces etc. in the build work zone:

1. Content Federation from S/4HANA system: In this we need to create the artifacts (Space, Pages, app etc.) in the S4 system and federate the content by making the content channel.

2. Local Artifcats in the Work Zone: Create Local Apps, Pages, spaces, groups etc. as required in the build work zone, standard edition.

 

We will be using the 2nd option as we are going to create local content in the work zone. Below are the configuration steps:

Step 1: Create App in the work zone

Access the Build Work Zone from the Sub account and got to Content Manager and create a local app.

Step 2: Configure the Transaction Code SMEN OR SESSION_MANAGER

If we configure the SAP Transaction Code in the app like we used to do in the Enterprise Portal, it wont work and give the below error "Transaction SMEN is locked":

 

After discussion with SAP Team, they told the Restriction to use the SAP Standard Transaction Code like SMEN OR SESSION_MANAGER

Direct access to SMEN (SAP Easy Access Menu) via SAP GUI for HTML from SAP Work Zone or FLP is not supported. This is not a limitation but a product and security decision:

SAP Security Note 2723570 explicitly disables access to SMEN in Web GUI because it exposes non–role-based system entry points, which is a security risk.
SAP Note 2786421 confirms that Web GUI actively blocks navigation to SMEN and redirects back to FLP.

FLP/Work Zone follows a role-based access model, which replaces the old Easy Access Menu by design. 

Step 3: 

Solution for the above restrcition is, open the webgui service in the app and it will try to access the SMEN code internally, you should give access to user for this tcode.

Below is the application screen shot created in the Work Zone locally:

Note: In the above screen shot I have uncheck the Check Box "Add intent and default SAP parameters to URL" just to keep the URL short. This is optional.

 

After completing the above configuration, now we can access the application in work zone:

 

 

I hope this blog will help people to access the Web GUI for HTML in the browser.

 

Regards,

Rohit Gera

entry.title: SAP Readiness Check - Easier SAP Note Implementation for Combined Scenarios
entry.content:
Following up on the “Combined scenarios” feature on the new SAP Readiness Check cloud application landing page (see blog New SAP Readiness Check Landing Page in SAP for Me), we aim to simplify customers’ change management efforts related to implementing the SAP Readiness Check data collectors.

Therefore, we enhanced the SAP Note Analyzer input files for SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA and SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgrades with the SAP Notes required for related SAP Readiness Check scenarios. For SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA, this includes the data collector notes for SAP Readiness Check for SAP ERP Usage and Data Profiling, as well as SAP Readiness Check for SAP SuccessFactors solutions. In the input file for SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgrades, we included the data collection note for SAP Readiness Check for SAP SuccessFactors solutions.

This extension supports customers:

  • Planning a combined project like transitioning from a SAP ERP ECC with HCM to SAP S/4HANA and SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Customers that are currently in an evaluation phase, trying to determine the best-fitting transition path to SAP S/4HANA – conversion, strategic new implementation, or a selective data transition.

In a one-time effort, customers can install all required SAP Notes for all related SAP Readiness Check scenarios from a single SAP Note Analyzer input file. This adaptation to the input files simplifies customers’ change management efforts as they can now easily combine all relevant SAP Notes into a single change request. Thereby, avoiding multiple rounds of approval and last-minute expedited change requests to fulfill service delivery prerequisites.

The SAP Note Analyzer input files are attached to SAP Note 3308130 – Note Analyzer Input File for SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA and SAP Note 3365856 - Note Analyzer Input File for SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgrades.

entry.title: Re: Issue – Purchase Requisition Creation for Lean Servic...
entry.content:
The root cause is that Lean Services (Product Type Group 2, material type SRV) require service‑specific fields (like Validity Start Date and Validity End Date) that are not exposed in Project Builder for components. Standard Project System integration with Enhanced Procurement Mode supports materials, but Lean Services behave differently because they are processed through the Service Procurement framework in MM.

The blog by @Mario_Franz  explains that Lean Service purchasing is integrated with PS networks, but it highlights that Lean Services follow the MM service procurement logic, which requires validity dates. This is why Project Builder does not yet expose those fields for service components Lean Service Enablement for Project Networks 

Recommended Solution

Short term: Generate PRs for Lean Services outside Project Builder (via MM/Fiori apps).

Medium term: Monitor SAP release notes and OSS notes for enhancements. SAP has indicated that Lean Service enablement in PS is being expanded, but full support (including validity date fields in Project Builder) is not yet delivered.

entry.title: Creating a ‘Hello World’ Joule Agent
entry.content:
Building on Michael Ameling's excellent Agent Builder in Joule Studio announcement and The Joule Studio Learning Series, this post will guide you through building your first Joule Agent.

I'll walk you through the steps to build a "Hello, World" agent. Along the way, you'll get familiar with the basic workflow in SAP Build, explore the UI, avoid at least one common pitfall, and have your agent up and running in just a few minutes. By building the simplest possible agent, you'll gain the confidence that building agents is easy, and creating more sophisticated ones will feel like a natural next step. Let's get started.

Setup and Prerequisites
The setup of SAP Build Apps, the activation of Joule, and the SAP Agent Builder are described in these two blog posts:
  • How to Get Started with Joule Studio
  • Activate Joule Studio in 5 Simple Steps

I'll assume that's done, and we'll start our journey in the lobby of SAP Build Apps.

Creating a Project
Click on the "Create" button, and select "Joule Skill":
 
 
Enter a name and a description:
 

Creating the Joule Agent

You'll be forwarded to the overview of your project where you can create the Joule Agent:

 

Again, enter a name and a description. A proposal for the identifier will be generated automatically:

 
On the details page of your Hello World Agent, you could fill in the fields manually, but let's use Joule to create a proposal. In the top left, click on the Joule icon to open the Joule sidebar. Enter a prompt like this:

"Can you please update our existing Hello World agent by filling in the expertise, instructions and additional context? The agent should respond to every prompt with "Hello, World!"."

 
 
As a result, Joule updates the agent, and we're almost done. We just need to add a placeholder tool, for example the calculator, because Joule Studio does not allow an agent to be saved without a tool assignment:

 
Now you can save the agent, and we can move on to testing the functionality we've just implemented.

 

Testing the Joule Agent

When you click on the "Test" button (just above the "Save" button), you'll be asked which environment you'd like to test in. Select the environment associated with your email address.

If your email address doesn't appear in the dropdown, you need to activate your private environment first. To do so, click on the SAP Build icon in the top left corner, click on "Control Tower" in the left-hand menu, and then click on the "Environments" tile:

On the Environments screen, click the button to activate your private environment:

Afterwards, you can resume testing and your private environment should appear in the dropdown as described above.

Now we're ready to test the agent, for example with a prompt like "Let's test the agent".

 
 
As we can see, the agent correctly responds with "Hello, World!" 😅

To end the test session, just click on the "Stop Testing" button.

Deploying the Joule Agent

To get our agent out of the testing environment, we need to deploy it to a real environment.

Let's create a Hello World environment. Navigate to the Control Tower, select the Environments tile, and click on "Create". Enter a name and description for the environment. The proposal for the identifier will be generated automatically.

 

Your user will be automatically added to this new environment. If you see a blank screen when you want to view the environment details, just try refreshing the page in the browser. It may take a little while to generate.

Once the environment is active, we can deploy our agent. Click on the "Release" button in the agent overview.

 

In the subsequent pop-up, enter a release note and click on "Release".

 

Once released, switch to version 1.0.0 at the top of the screen by clicking on the dropdown next to the title.

 
 

In the released version, there is a "Deploy" button instead of the "Test" and "Release" buttons. Click on the "Deploy" button, select the Hello World environment, and click on "Deploy".

 
 
After a few moments, the status of agent version 1.0.0 will change to "Deployed and Active".

Testing the Deployment

As a final step in this tutorial, let's test our deployed agent in the Hello World environment.

Open the Hello World environment via the Control Tower and view the environment details by clicking on it in the list. There, you will see the deployment of the Hello World agent we just made.

To test it, open the Joule tab for this environment. Depending on your screen resolution, this may be hidden in the "More" drop-down. On this tab, click the "Launch" button.

 

Joule will open in a new tab and we can prompt it to test the agent, for example like this: "Run the Hello World agent."

To confirm that Joule actually called the agent, you can investigate the log by clicking on the icon in the top right of the title bar.

 
 

Conclusion

Congratulations, you just implemented your first Joule agent! As intended, the overall result is "just" a Hello World agent, but this is an important first step in your journey to becoming a Joule agent builder. You familiarized yourself with the UI and performed the full workflow from start to finish, including creating a project, creating an agent, testing, and deploying it. Along the way, we also used Joule itself to fill in the agent's configuration details.

From here, there are many more topics worth exploring, like Joule skills, MCP integration, or agent-to-agent communication. However, for me, it's always important to build a Hello World example first without getting lost in the details. With more complex starter scenarios, it's easy to get stuck on technical details and lose sight of the big picture. Now that you've completed your "Hello World", you can iteratively add more features and build more sophisticated agents. What's the first use case you're going to tackle?

entry.title: Opportunities for women
entry.content:
Dear SAP team 
Joule SAP HANA Enterprise Cloud 
I am writing to express my strong interest in building a professional career in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, along with BTP and to kindly request guidance regarding free, accessible training or certification opportunities.
I come from a region with limited professional and educational opportunities, which is why I pursued my SAP studies online through a German institution. I financed my SAP courses, and certification entirely on my own because I am deeply committed to developing a long-term career within the SAP ecosystem.
Despite my dedication, I am currently facing a challenge. My current employer does not provide exposure to SAP S/4HANA Cloud projects or support for further certifications. Additionally, recent changes to the SAP certification model make it difficult for independent learners like myself to maintain credentials without a paid subscription.
However, I remain fully determined to continue growing in this field. I am not looking for shortcuts, only for an opportunity to strengthen my foundational knowledge in SAP S/4HANA Cloud solutions and prove my capability. Access to structured learning resources, a bootcamp, or guidance toward an entry pathway would allow me to contribute meaningfully and represent SAP solutions in regions where opportunities are limited.
My goal is not only to advance personally, but to become a strong ambassador for SAP technologies and help expand SAP's impact globally. I am prepared to invest my time, discipline, and long-term commitment to achieve this.
 
My other interests lie strongly in artificial intelligence and in exploring how we can push new boundaries responsibly. Using its advanced capability without compromising data integrity or knowledge quality. I dedicate a significant amount of my own time to testing and evaluating AI tools because I genuinely enjoy understanding how they work and how they can be applied.
In my current role, however, the use of AI is limited and not fully encouraged, which makes it challenging to bring that expertise into my day‑to‑day work. I’m motivated to contribute more in this area and to help shape thoughtful, secure, and innovative AI adoption.
 
I would sincerely appreciate any advice, scholarship information, free courses/bootcamps or any other direction you could provide.
 
Thank you for your time and consideration.
 
Kind regards,  
Elisabeta

entry.title: Quick Data Backup for SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
SAP Commerce Cloud introduces Quick Data Backup, a streamlined backup option designed for situations where speed and simplicity matter more than long-term retention.

The feature complements the existing standard Data Backup and is intended for short-lived safety checkpoints, for example before a deployment, during troubleshooting, or when testing changes in an environment.

What Quick Data Backup is for

Quick Data Backup focuses on the Commerce database and uses Azure Point-in-Time Restore (PITR) to create backups quickly and restore them reliably.

Typical use cases include:

  • Creating a short-term safety snapshot before a deployment

  • Capturing database state before configuration or data changes

  • Restoring an environment to a recent known-good state during troubleshooting

Quick Data Backups are time-limited and automatically expire after 35 days, helping to prevent unnecessary storage growth.

Current scope and limitation

Included: Commerce database
Not included: Media storage

This initial version intentionally targets the most critical and most frequently restored component, which is the database.

Backup limits and changes

With the introduction of Quick Data Backup, backup limits are adjusted as follows.

Standard Data Backups

  • Production environments: maximum 3 backups

  • Non-production environments: maximum 2 backups

Quick Data Backups

  • All environment types: maximum 4 backups

These limits help reduce long-term storage usage while keeping sufficient flexibility for both short-term and long-term backup scenarios.

How it works in the Cloud Portal

Select backup type
In the Cloud Portal, choose Quick Data Backup as an alternative to the standard Data Backup when creating a new backup

View created Quick Data Backups
Once the Quick Data Backup is created, it appears in the list of backups together with its expiration date.

Quick Database Restore without creating a backup
You can also restore the database directly without creating a Quick Data Backup first. Use the Quick DB Restore button on the main backup page.

 

Select restore point and target environment
Choose the point in time you want to restore the database to, up to 35 days back, and select the target environment.

 

When to use which backup

Use Quick Data Backup when:

  • You need a fast, short-term backup

  • Long-term retention is not required

  • Media data does not need to be restored

Use standard Data Backup when:

  • You need long-term retention

  • You require database and media consistency

Further information

For full details on backup limits, behavior, and supported scenarios, refer to the official SAP Commerce Cloud documentation:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_CLOUD_PUBLIC_CLOUD/0fa6bcf4736c46f78c248512391eb467/f777fc0a4c524e7aa20d84fc8a55b171.html

entry.title: Re: Switching from domain knowledge to consultant role
entry.content:
Your background is a strong foundation for moving into an SAP functional consultant role. You already have the domain expertise in SCM, hands‑on SAP MM knowledge, and experience supporting implementation and UATs. The transition is about reframing your profile and gaining structured exposure to the consultant’s way of working.

How the transition works

Domain expertise first: Your 12 years in SCM front‑end (RFP/RFQ, negotiations, PO handling, vendor management) are your biggest asset. Clients want consultants who understand procurement realities.

Functional consultant role: You act as the bridge between business and system. You gather requirements, design processes, validate solutions, and support testing. Configuration is part of the toolkit, but not the only skill.

Configuration knowledge: While you won’t be expected to configure everything from day one, you should understand the basics (release strategies, pricing, account determination, purchasing document types). This helps you speak the same language as technical consultants and know what’s feasible.

Recommended approach

Strengthen process + system mapping
Learn how business requirements translate into SAP functionality. Practice writing functional specifications (FS) for developers.

Get exposure to configuration
You don’t need to master every IMG node, but knowing the key customizing areas in MM will make you credible. Think of configuration knowledge as “awareness + ability to test,” not “daily job.”

Focus on consulting skills
Requirement gathering, fit‑gap analysis, UAT preparation, issue resolution. Documentation and communication with both business and technical teams.

Quality landscape experience
As a consultant, you’ll test configuration in quality, validate end‑to‑end scenarios, and guide users. You’ll be the one ensuring that what’s configured matches business needs before it moves to production.

Next step

Your best move is to combine your SCM domain expertise with structured SAP MM functional knowledge. That means:

  • Learn configuration basics (not to become a configurator, but to understand the system’s levers).
  • Build consulting skills (requirements, FS, UAT, issue resolution).
  • Position yourself as a domain expert who can now functionally consult on SAP MM.

entry.title: Re: Who and how to create or configure HSN code
entry.content:
Hi @BANDLAVENKATESH ,
Normally HSN Codes is created by Functional Consultant (FI/MM/SD). Typically responsible for configuring HSN codes in SAP, since it impacts tax determination and material master data. Business/Tax Team: Provides the correct HSN code and slab details (GST % rates, exemptions, etc.).
Basis/Technical Team: Not directly involved, unless enhancements or custom tables are required.

Please find below the step‑by‑step process to create HSN in SAP
1. Create HSN Code in SAP
Use Transaction Code: J1ID
Navigate to Chapter IDs → Create new entry
Enter:HSN Code (numeric code provided by tax team)
Description (material category)
Tax Rates/Slabs (CGST, SGST, IGST percentages)

2. Assign HSN Code to Material
Use Transaction Code: MM02 (Change Material Master)
Go to Sales: Sales Org. Data 1 or Foreign Trade: Export Data tab (depending on version)
Enter the HSN Code in the relevant field
Save changes

3. Maintain Condition Records
Use Transaction Code: VK11
Select the relevant Condition Type (e.g., GST condition types like JGST, JCGST, JSGST, JIGST)
Enter: HSN Code
Tax Rate/Slab
Validity period
Save condition record

4. Testing
Create a Sales Order/Invoice
System should automatically pick the correct tax rate based on the HSN code assigned to the material.

You must always confirm the correct HSN code with the tax compliance team before configuration.

Hope I answer your question.

entry.title: Re: Lean Service Enablement for Project Networks
entry.content:
Hi @Mario_Franz,

First of all, I would like to express my appreciation for this comprehensive documentation.                                                                                                                           

We are currently encountering an issue when generating Purchase Requisitions from components in SAP Project System for Lean Services. The scenario concerns materials of type SRV with Product Type Group 2 (Lean Services), within a project and network configured in Enhanced Procurement Mode. This mode has been correctly activated at both the Project Profile level and the Network/Plant level.

The first issue observed is the absence of any option related to services in the Project Builder user-specific settings. When navigating to Project Builder and accessing the user-specific options, only standard processable objects such as materials and activities are available. No setting allows services or lean services to be defined as processable objects.

The second issue occurs during the creation of a Purchase Requisition from a material component using Lean Service ( materials of type SRV with Product Type Group 2 ). When attempting to generate the PR, the system displays an error message requesting the entry of a “Validity Start Date.” However, this field is not available or visible in the Project Builder interface for either the component or the related network activity, making it impossible for the user to provide the required information.

This behavior is observed exclusively for Lean Services, as non stocked material components do not trigger the same error and function correctly.                  

Could you please advise if there might be something missing in my configuration that is preventing this functionality on my system?

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: PLMSI 3.0 FP04 – What You Need to Know About the Latest Release
entry.content:
Another year has started and we have seen the birth of another feature pack for PLM System Integration for SAP S/4HANA.

Find out what's new and why it matters.

Extended characteristic mapping for Product and Document exchange outbound scenario

Release 3.0 FP04 introduces a more flexible outbound mapping layer for product versions (PV) and document versions (DV).
In addition to class-based characteristic exchange, consultants can now configure:

  • Outbound class assignments, including class numbers and class types, per product version
  • Mappings of class characteristics to target fields in the Domain Model, or to extension fields of the product version
  • Conversion routines, default values, and value mapping tables

This gives solution architects a finer‑grained control mechanism to harmonize master data between SAP and external PLM systems, which proves very useful in multi-system engineering landscapes.

For more information, see Map DM Product Version to SAP Material Master.

Likewise, this feature is available for Document Version Integration. 

For more information, see Map DM Document Version to SAP Document Info Record.

Outbound maintenance requests for classification changes

FP04 adds the ability to trigger outbound maintenance requests for PV as material master, DV as document info record, CO as change request, and CO as engineering change master, based on:

  • Class assignments
  • Characteristic value updates
  • Value changes 

Change Object Integration: In the new view Define Classification Conditions for Outbound in the Customizing for Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Master or Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Record, you can now control which characteristics trigger outbound data transfer to an external PLM system when updated in the SAP system. Specify a Class Number, Class Type, and Characteristic to define outbound-relevant classification conditions.

For more information, see Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Master or Map DM Change Object to SAP Change Record.

The same feature is available for Document Version Integration (see Map DM Document Version to SAP Document Info Record) and for Product Version Integration (see Map DM Product Version to SAP Material Master).

For integration architects, this significantly improves synchronization accuracy and reduces the chances of missed updates for downstream processes.

Product and structure version compatibility support in version-controlled BOMs

 
PLMSI now supports product versioning at both header and item level for version-controlled BOMs.
With the new BOM Header Unlimited Validity checkbox, consultants can enable perpetual header validity (defaulting to Jan 1, 1900), simplifying BOM maintenance and reducing the need for repeated header creation. This is particularly relevant for customers with high-frequency engineering iterations and complex BOM structures.
 

For more information, see Map DM Structure Version to SAP Classic BOM.

 

Transfer of iPPE: PVS from an external PLM system

Feature Pack 04 expands structure integration capabilities by adding support for transferring Integrated Product and Process Engineering (iPPE) – Product Variant Structures (PVS). You can now configure inbound processing for:

  • Access nodes & variants
  • Structure nodes & node relationships
  • Component variants

A new integration pattern (SAP_SVasPPECMPPVS) and several supporting BAdIs provide robust extension points for customizing the mapping logic. 

For more information, see Map DM Structure Version to SAP CMP Product Variant Structure.

What Else is New in PLM system integration for SAP S/4HANA?

The release delivers additional BAdIs across POL, SV, and change handling scenarios.
Notably, you can now leverage the MPO Value Control Structure, which boosts flexibility when working with extension fields and custom mappings. For teams implementing tailored integration logic, this significantly improves maintainability and robustness. For detailed information, see our What's New Viewer. Please make sure to select the latest version 3.0 FP04 to filter for the most recent updates.

Expand Your Range

We are happy and proud that another members of our PLM system integration family has released a new versions of its product:

PLM system integration, add-on for 3DEXPERIENCE has published its new version 3.3. Check out the Release Notes.

Business Content

As for each feature pack, we have updated our comprehensive set of business content (BC) sets to help you configure your system with ease. For details, see Initial Configuration.

For a comprehensive list of our new and enhanced features for release 3.0 FP04, check out our What's New Viewer.

All relevant information on PLM system integration is also available via the links below:

  • SAP Help Portal: PLM system integration for SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Product Availability Matrix (SAP login required): Matrix for PLM system integration 3.0 for SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Roadmap Explorer: PLM system integration

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo

entry.title: Automating Invoice Predictions from SAP Databricks with SAP Datasphere Task Chain
entry.content:
In our previous blog, we demonstrated how to process semi-structured PDF invoices, parse them and store the results in a Delta table before applying machine learning algorithms to predict payment delays. These predictions are hosted in SAP Databricks as part of a customer managed data product, with results written to a target Delta table in Unity Catalog and shared via Delta Sharing with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC).

This is how our customer managed data product holding the first processed 1.000 invoices looks like before any updates: 

The next challenge on our journey was automation. Once new invoices land in the S3 bucket, we need a fully automated process that parses them, updates predictions, and refreshes the Delta share, without any manual intervention. Since SAP consumes the data federatively, business users expect up‑to‑date predictions in near real time.

To meet this requirement, my colleague @Mostafa_Shaarawy1 and Idesigned and implemented an end‑to‑end orchestration flow using SAP Datasphere (DSP) Task Chains and Databricks API to:
  • trigger the Databricks notebook on schedule or on demand,
  • monitor its execution,
  • and ensure updated predictions flow instantly to SAP BDC.

To achieve this, we are going to utilize the run-now and the get run APIs described in Databricks API reference, with authentication handled via a Databricks Service Principle.

The result: a fully automated pipeline that delivers fresh, reliable insights to business users, improving decision‑making, operational efficiency, and payment‑risk visibility.

Business Use Case: Finance Manager’s Perspective

Imagine you’re a finance manager responsible for optimizing cash flow. You rely on accurate predictions of payment delays to decide which customers to follow up with and when. With Datasphere orchestration, the moment new invoices arrive, the system automatically processes them, updates predictions, and makes the latest insights available in SAP BDC, without any manual steps. This means you can act faster, reduce overdue payments, and improve working capital, all while saving time and effort.

We’ll achieve this using Datasphere Task Chains, leveraging their API Task (REST) capability and a Generic HTTP connection to seamlessly integrate with SAP Databricks.

Disclaimer: The scenario we are covering in this blog is technically working fine but is not officially supported in the SAP Databricks documentation.

Solution Overview

The architecture looks like this:

What makes this work?

  • The architecture consists of three components:

    • DSP Task Chains orchestrate Databricks jobs using REST API calls.
    • Databricks Jobs API triggers and monitors the notebook execution.
    • Delta Sharing + Unity Catalog exposes the updated prediction table live to SAP BDC/DSP.

This ensures new data flows from invoice ingestion → prediction → Delta share → SAP consumption end‑to‑end, without manual steps.

Prerequisites

Before building the orchestration, we need to first complete these steps:

  1. Create a Service Principal in SAP Databricks Workspace at the workspace level to generate:

    • Client ID
    • Client Secret
    • OAuth Token Endpoint.

  2. Assign the service principal permissions to run the job and access the target Delta table.

    • This avoids using personal tokens and ensures secure automated API calls.

  3. Prepare the Databricks Job

    • Identify the Job ID of the notebook that:
      • Processes new invoices,
      • Runs ML predictions,
      • Writes results into the target Delta table in Unity Catalog.

  4. Update the Delta Share and the Share CSN document

Step-by-Step Implementation

1) Set-up Databricks and create service Principal

A service principal in Databricks is a dedicated, non-human identity built for automation. It enables secure, API‑only access to Databricks resources for scripts, automated workflows, and CI/CD pipelines, all without depending on personal user credentials.

a- Go to Settings > Ìdentity and Access > Service Principals > Add service principal:

b- Generate a secret for this Service Principal and copy it as well as the Client ID (you'll need them for the definition of the Httpconnection in

SAP Datasphere😞

c- Go to the notebook under Scheduleand create a new one:

d- Switch to the Jobs & Pipelines section and select your scheduled job and copy the job ID (you'll need it in the API Invocationrequest body of the task chain in SAP Datasphere to call the right job): 

2) Set-up your Http Connection in your Datasphere space

  • In your space, navigate to Connections in Datasphere and choose Http as connection type.

  • Enter the connection details.
Host   = <your_tenant_url without 'https://' >.
Port    = 443
Protocol     = HTTPS

Use Cloud Connector
    = False

Authentication Type
     = OAuth2.0

OAuth Grant Type
 = Client Credentials

OAuth Token Endpoint = <Your_token_Url_endpoint>

OAuth Scope = all-apis

OAuth Response Type: Token

OAuth Token Request Content Type: URL Encoded

Client ID: <Client_Id_of_DBX_Service_Principle>

Secret: <Secret_of_DBX_Service_Principle>

  • Save the connection.

3) Create Task Chain with API Task

  • Navigate to Data builder and create a new Task Chain.

  • From the top of the canvas, drag and drop API Task to the editor.

From the properties of the Task Chain, select under Connection the Http connection created in step 2.

In the API Invocation section, choose POST method and the mode to be Asynchronous.

  • The base url will be automatically generated from the connection details
  • In the API Path, write run-now (more information can be found in the Databricks API documentation https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/jobs/runnow).
  • The body should be a json object with the job-idof the Databricks job.
  • Choose Get result from HTTP status code and response body and set the path to run_id. This will map the property run_id in the API response to be used for status retrieval to be able to monitor the Databricks job from the task chain.

In the Status API section, we will use the get single run API for status retrieval as documented in Databricks API documentation https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/jobs/getrun.

  • Set the API path to runs/get?run_id={id}. Since we have, in the previous step, configured the path to run-id, the API task will dynamically use its value in the query parameter during run time.

  • In the response section choose Get result from HTTP status code and response body to determine whether the databricks job was successful or not, based on the result from the response body.
  • As success indicator use: status.termination_details.code equals to SUCCESS
  • As error indicator use: status.stateequals to TERMINATED

 

4) Run the API task to trigger the Databricks job

Now the API task is ready to be triggered. Once triggered the Invocation API will be called with the job-id in the request body and the status API will be called periodically to evaluate the completion of the Databricks job.

For testing purpose, you can conveniently just run the API task without having to run the task chain. Click on Test Run in the API task to run it.

You can now click on the Data Integration Monitor icon to see the run details.

When you switch to the monitoring, you can then see the run details of the job:

This is also reflected in SAP Databricks:

Once the job has completed successfully, you can see the 5 invoices newly added to the S3 bucket now updated in the custom managed data product shared from SAP Databricks to SAP Business Data Cloud:

Key Benefits for Business Users

  • Real-time insights: Data is refreshed immediately as new invoices are processed.
  • Zero manual effort: A fully automated end‑to‑end pipeline.
  • Improved cash flow management: Faster decisions supported by fresh insights.
  • Operational efficiency: No delays between data processing and business consumption.
  • Federated access: Data is consumed directly in SAP BDC—no physical copies or complex integrations required.

entry.title: Re: FAGL_FCV-Posting period 002 2026 is not open
entry.content: Hi, Please run transaction OB52 and check: Which authorization group is open for period 002/2026. Whether company code 1600 is included in that group. If you confirm that OB52 is open for 1600 but the error persists, then the issue is likely with valuation area assignment or fiscal year variant mismatch.

entry.title: Re: Working with SAP SDK for Android and older Android devices with DigiCert G5 Certificate
entry.content:
We were already aware of potential issues due to the missing G5 certificate, as we recently received reports from a few users with older Android versions who were no longer able to access the SAP Build Work Zone. In these cases, access is provided directly through a web browser. We were able to solve the problem here by having users install the new DigiCert G5 certificate locally on their devices.

After consulting with our Customer Success Manager, SAP also considers this to be a correct solution for cases where the certificate cannot be stored directly in the app itself, as described in this post. Maybe this information will help someone else.

entry.title: Value differences are posted solely to stock account in multiple line invoices
entry.content:
Hi,

in sap, for invoice with multiple line items, line by line quantity is checked against the stock in the time of posting.

This leads to that if there are differences they are posted to stock account instead of the line quantities being accumulated and then posted proportionally to stock account and expense.

How did you tackle this issue in your company?

Thanks,

Golan

entry.title: Re: Italian special depreciation keys for ledger 2L
entry.content:
Hi,

  1. Parallel valuation done by ledgers assumes differences in valuation. Any attempt to synchronize multiple GAAP cross ledgers, e.g. by using the same depreciation keys, does not make any sense in general. 
  2. Especially the depreciation keys in Italy are "special" and typically not applicable in IFRS.. 
  3. Entity "Chart of Depreciation" does not play any vital role in Intelligent Asset Accounting anymore and it will be completely removed in the future.
  4. You can create custom depreciation keys also for IFRS via the process of pre-approved Expert Configuration process via template DPK.

Best regards,

   Mick
